summary:
The accessibility prompt is one of the cleanest in the corpus. Baseline scorecard is perfect on what it tests: bad (img-no-alt) flagged high 3/3 (recall 1.0), good (accessible-img) clean 3/3 (fp 0.0). The template codebase itself sets a HIGH a11y bar (role="alert", aria-label, semantic
/

/
    /
  • ) and ships NO , NO div-onClick, NO unlabeled inputs — so there is no codebase-bar violation and effectively zero surface for the prompt to over-flag template idioms. The real weakness is fixture COVERAGE: the prompt enumerates 3 'high' categories + 1 'info' category but the corpus exercises only ONE (missing accessible name / alt) with a single good+bad pair. Recommended edits are light-touch: rubric-align the severity language, add fixtures for the two untested high categories.

severity_bar_issues.issue: Blanket 'high' for all three categories is mostly defensible but slightly stricter than the draft rubric for one sub-case.
severity_bar_issues.detail: Rubric defines high = 'blocks a builder ... incorrect behavior ... or a broken contract.' Missing alt, div-onClick-as-button, and no-keyboard-handler all genuinely break behavior for assistive-tech users, so 'high' is correct for those. The borderline case is an icon-only button missing aria-label on a NON-critical control: that is arguably 'medium' (degraded, not broken). Low priority — keep 'high' as the default but allow the agent to step down to medium when the affected control is non-essential.

severity_bar_issues.issue: Prompt has no medium/low rungs — it only emits 'high' or 'info'.
severity_bar_issues.detail: This bimodal scale (high|info) predates the shared rubric's four-rung ladder (high/medium/low/info). It is not causing false positives today, but it prevents the agent from expressing the 'should fix before merge but does not block' tier the rubric defines.

scope_creep_note: The prompt has a clean, explicit negative scope ('Do NOT flag backend/Python changes, or purely stylistic CSS with no a11y impact'), which correctly fences out the api-design/usability/observability-fe agents' territory. No scope-creep observed in the baseline findings.
hallucination_note: No hallucinated or over-reaching findings in the baseline. All 3 bad-case findings cite the actual line (30/31 in the rendered file) with accurate WCAG 1.1.1 grounding. Good case correctly returns [].
codebase_bar_note:
No violation. Template frontend (Items.tsx, App.tsx) ALREADY uses role="alert", aria-label="items", and semantic
/

/
    /
  • /

    . It ships no , no onClick-div, no unlabeled . The prompt holds new code to a bar the template meets or exceeds — there is no stricter-than-codebase problem, and no template idiom that would false-positive.
internal_consistency_note: Single-pattern fixtures give no opportunity to observe an A-vs-B inconsistency; baseline shows none. The prompt's rules are mutually consistent (each category maps to one severity).

fixture_verdicts.rationale:
Renders with a descriptive accessible name; differs from the bad fixture ONLY by the presence of alt= (confirmed by diff). Genuinely clean for the defect under test. No latent secondary defect (the surrounding
/

are non-interactive and need no role).

Renders with NO alt attribute — an unambiguous WCAG 2.1 SC 1.1.1 (Non-text Content) failure. Well-formed patch (11 added lines, 0 removed, applies cleanly). expect.json seeds frontend/src/Items.tsx, matching the patched file. Recall 1.0 across 3 repeats.

fixture_coverage_gaps.untested_category: Non-semantic interactive elements (<div>/<span> with onClick used as button/link, no role + keyboard handler)
fixture_coverage_gaps.note: Enumerated in the prompt but no fixture exercises it. This is the highest-value gap to fill — it is also the Plan-18 over-flagging risk surface (an agent could flag legitimate non-interactive divs). A good fixture (proper <button>) + bad fixture (onClick <div>) would both validate recall AND guard against fp.

fixture_coverage_gaps.untested_category: Keyboard inaccessibility (custom interactive control with no onKeyDown / not focusable / missing tabIndex)
fixture_coverage_gaps.note: Enumerated but untested. Needs a good (focusable, keyboard-handled control) + bad (mouse-only custom control) pair.

fixture_coverage_gaps.untested_category: Missing accessible names — icon-only button without aria-label, and form <input> without <label>/aria-label
fixture_coverage_gaps.note: Only the alt sub-case is covered. The icon-button and unlabeled-input sub-cases are untested.

fixture_coverage_gaps.untested_category: ARIA/contrast smells (misused/invalid aria-*, hardcoded low-contrast colors)
fixture_coverage_gaps.note: Untested; lowest priority since it is info-only.

proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.change: Inherit the shared rubric severity ladder explicitly and allow a medium rung.
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.before: - Missing accessible names: an without alt, an icon-only button with no aria-label, a form with no associated
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.after: - Missing accessible names: an without alt, an icon-only button with no aria-label, a form with no associated
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.rationale: Aligns with the draft rubric's high/medium distinction without weakening the dominant case. COUPLED with no fixture change (existing fixtures stay high).

proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.change: Add an explicit codebase-bar / negative-example sentence to prevent future over-flagging of non-interactive divs.
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.before: Do NOT flag backend/Python changes, or purely stylistic CSS with no a11y impact.
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.after:
Do NOT flag backend/Python changes, or purely stylistic CSS with no a11y impact. A plain
/ with NO onClick/keyboard/role is a layout element and is NOT an a11y defect — only flag interactive elements that lack semantics. Do not require alt on elements that are not .
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.rationale: Pre-empts the Plan-18 over-flagging mechanism the moment the onClick-div fixtures land. COUPLED with the new fixtures below.

proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.bad: A <div onClick={...}> used as a button with no role/tabIndex/onKeyDown — expect one 'high' finding on the div line.
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.good: A proper <button onClick={...}> (or a <div role="button" tabIndex={0} onClick onKeyDown>) — expect [].
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.rationale: Validates the prompt's first 'high' category AND guards fp on legitimate layout divs (good fixture should also include an inert <div> wrapper that must NOT be flagged).

proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.bad: A custom clickable control (e.g. span with onClick) with no onKeyDown and no tabIndex — expect 'high'.
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.good: Same control made focusable + keyboard-operable (tabIndex={0} + onKeyDown) — expect [].

proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.change: Add bad fixture: icon-only button without aria-label and/or form <input> without label.
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.bad: An icon-only <button><Icon/></button> with no aria-label (and/or a bare <input>) — expect 'high'.
proposed_prompt_fixture_edits.rationale: Covers the missing-accessible-name sub-cases beyond alt.

proposed_block_threshold.rationale: The agent emits only 'high' (true defects) and 'info' (advisory). Blocking on 'high' is correct and matches the baseline: the one bad fixture must block, the one good must pass. With perfect baseline precision (fp 0.0) and recall (1.0) and a template that gives no over-flag surface, there is no signal to raise the bar. Set block_threshold = high; info/medium/low remain non-blocking. Re-confirm after the proposed onClick-div fixtures land (Phase 2/3 re-sweep) — that is the category most likely to surface a false positive.
